input.headline: Market town with 12,000 residents named Britain's least affordable

raw.headline: Market town with 12,000 residents named Britain's least affordable

winner: The town of Affordably was thrilled to be named Britain’s most affordable—until they realized it was a typo and everyone else had already moved out.

candidates.text: Why did the market town's residents start investing in stocks instead of groceries? They were tired of being *priced out* of both!
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Puns on 'priced out' tie the joke to the headline's context with clever wordplay, showing specificity and surprise."}

candidates.text: I tried to buy a house in Britain's least affordable town, but all I could afford was a shed... and even that had a "No Shedding" policy.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Surprising twist with 'No Shedding' policy, specific to housing affordability, concise and natural humor."}

candidates.text: When I tried to sell my house in Britain's most expensive village—where a single croissant costs £50—I was told, 'You can't afford the postage.'
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Surprising twist on affordability, specific reference to a croissant price, concise and natural humor"}

candidates.text: The town is so expensive that the residents are literally "out of pocket" just for living there.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Puns on 'out of pocket' are clever and specific to the context, with a good balance of surprise and natural English."}

candidates.text: The town tried everything to become more affordable—so they renamed themselves "Britain's Most Affordable," and suddenly everyone wanted to live there.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat clever with the twist of renaming, but it lacks specificity to the town's situation and feels a bit generic in its approach."}

candidates.text: The town of Affordably was thrilled to be named Britain’s most affordable—until they realized it was a typo and everyone else had already moved out.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat creative with the typo twist, but 'Affordably' doesn't align well with the headline's 'least affordable.' The humor feels forced and lacks specificity to the town or situation described."}

candidates.text: The town was so unaffordable that the local residents were forced to start living in the houses they owned, just to save on rent.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat surprising and specific, but the humor falls flat due to a lack of clarity and awkward phrasing. The concept of 'living in the houses they owned' to save on rent is confusing and doesn't clearly connect to affordability."}

candidates.text: The local government tried to lower the cost of living by rebranding the town as "Expensive," but residents are protesting because they can't afford the new signs.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat surprising and specific, but the humor is weak and relies on a tired rebranding trope. The explanation feels forced and lacks punch."}

candidates.text: Britain's most affordable market town, with a population of just one resident, announced today that it had officially run out of cheap options.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat surprising and specific, but it misrepresents the original headline and introduces a fictional scenario that doesn't clearly connect to the affordability issue."}
